title: Tale of Two Ethiopias | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Ethiopia's foregone-conclusion 2026 election will be read through two opposing lenses: a frontier-market growth story versus a fragile state riven by insurgencies, an unimplemented Tigray peace deal, and hostile neighbors. The author argues bullish investor optimism understates serious instability risks.

insights.claim: Bullish investors cite Ethiopia's high growth rates and the opening of long-closed sectors such as telecom in the world's tenth most populous country as a frontier-market success story in the making.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Telecom and sector liberalization are the concrete entry points for frontier-market capital; size positions against political-risk discounting.
insights.quote: Bullish investors point to Ethiopia’s high growth rates and the appealing opportunities that come with opening up long-closed sectors like telecom in the world’s tenth most populus country.

insights.claim: Data validity problems in Ethiopia have left the World Bank unable to classify the country's income level, even as bullish investors remain undeterred.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Unreliable national statistics mean headline growth figures should be heavily discounted in any investment thesis.
insights.quote: questions around data validity that have left the World Bank unable to classify the country’s income level

insights.claim: Addis Ababa's rapid physical transformation into soaring towers, wide roads, and a world-class airport hub was achieved through the displacement of tens of thousands of citizens.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Visible capital-city progress can mislead due-diligence; the social cost signals governance and unrest risk beneath the showcase.
insights.quote: The city’s rapid transformation—which involved the displacement of tens of thousands of citizens—has delivered soaring towers

insights.claim: The 2022 Pretoria Agreement that ended the Tigray civil war has not been implemented, fueling speculation about a return to war with Eritrea potentially switching sides.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: An unimplemented peace deal plus possible Eritrea realignment is a renewed-war catalyst that could reprice all Ethiopian risk.
insights.quote: The 2022 Pretoria Agreement that ended the devastating civil war in Tigray has not been implemented, leading to months of speculation about a return to war, this time with Eritrea switching sides.
insights.locator: What a visit to the capital

insights.claim: Insurgencies in Ethiopia's Amhara and Oromia regions persist despite federal efforts to suppress them, and a national dialogue on ethnic federalism has failed to earn public trust.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Active multi-region insurgencies undercut the stability premise of the growth story; track conflict spread beyond Tigray.
insights.quote: Insurgencies in Amhara and Oromia persist despite federal efforts to quash them.
insights.locator: What a visit to the capital

insights.claim: Independent media in Ethiopia has been shut down and journalists are subject to detention without charge, obscuring the scale of domestic unrest from outside observers.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Suppressed reporting means on-the-ground risk is systematically underreported; assume worse-than-visible instability.
insights.quote: journalists are subject to detention without charge and independent media outlets have been shut down
insights.locator: What a visit to the capital

insights.claim: Ethiopia's relations with neighbors are strained on multiple fronts: Abiy's push for sea access alarms Eritrea, support for Sudan's Rapid Support Forces has alienated Khartoum, Egypt protests the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam, and ties with Somalia remain marked by mutual suspicion.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: A four-way set of regional disputes, especially over Nile waters and sea access, is a standing external-conflict risk to development.
insights.quote: Egypt continues to express dire concern about the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam and control of the Nile waters.

insights.claim: The author argues that taking Ethiopia's future seriously requires embracing complexity, and that the election results were a foregone conclusion that shed little light on the country's direction.
insights.speaker: Michelle Gavin
insights.so_what: Treat the election as a non-event for the thesis; peace and social cohesion, not vote tallies, are the variables that gate the upside.
insights.quote: a genuine desire to see Ethiopia’s vast potential unlocked sooner rather than later cannot erase the salience of peace and social cohesion
